coredata.dc:description: As a new type of quantum dots (QDs), hexagonal boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) exhibit promising potential in the applications of disease diagnosis, fluorescence imaging, biosensing, metal ion detection, and so on, because of their remarkable chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and outstanding photoluminescence properties. However, the large-scale fabrication of homogeneous BNQDs still remains challenging. In this article, the properties and common fabrication methods of BNQDs are summarized based on the recent research progress. Then, the corresponding yields, morphologies, and fabrication mechanisms of these as-obtained BNQDs are discussed in detail. Moreover, the applications of these as-obtained BNQDs in different fields are also discussed. This article is expected to inspire new methods and improvements to achieve large-scale fabrication of homogeneous BNQDs, which will enable their practical applications in future.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a new type of quantum dots (QDs), hexagonal boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) exhibit promising potential in the applications of disease diagnosis, fluorescence imaging, biosensing, metal ion detection, and so on, because of their remarkable chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and outstanding photoluminescence properties. However, the large-scale fabrication of homogeneous BNQDs still remains challenging. In this article, the properties and common fabrication methods of BNQDs are summarized based on the recent research progress. Then, the corresponding yields, morphologies, and fabrication mechanisms of these as-obtained BNQDs are discussed in detail. Moreover, the applications of these as-obtained BNQDs in different fields are also discussed. This article is expected to inspire new methods and improvements to achieve large-scale fabrication of homogeneous BNQDs, which will enable their practical applications in future.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) with a size of less than 10 nm is a kind of novel zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterials. It can be synthesized by chemical assembly or fabricated by physical tailoring of two-dimensional (2D) layered structures. Due to the synergetic effect of size and edge defects, QDs exhibit many unique properties, making them be promising candidates in a variety of applications [1], such as bioimaging [2–7], biosensing [5,7,8], cancer cell therapy [9,10], photoelectrons [11,12], light-emitting diode (LED) technology [13,14], photocatalysis [15,16], and sensing [6,17]. However, the application of traditional semiconductive QDs, for example, zinc sulfide, cadmium sulfide, cadmium telluride, and zinc selenide, In biomarker and medical fields is greatly limited because these QDs usually contain heavy metal elements, which are very harmful to human health and environment [18,19]. Therefore, the development of metal-free QDs with favorable biocompatibility, low toxicity, and environmental friendliness has become one of the research hotspots in recent years.

Metal-free QDs, such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [20,21] and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [22–24], have been continuously developed in recent years owing to their excellent physical and chemical properties. The progress of CQDs and GQDs has greatly promoted the research enthusiasm of other two-dimensional graphene-like QDs [25], such as hexagonal boron nitride QDs (BNQDs), black phosphorus QDs [26], transition metal sulfide QDs, transition metal oxide QDs. In particular, the BNQDs, as an analogue of GQDs (Fig. 1 ), have become one of the attractive QDs in consideration of the unique properties of h-BN [27], such as outstanding electrical insulation [28–30], unique optical properties [31], excellent chemical and thermal stability [32,33], high thermal conductivity [34–36], remarkable mechanical strength [37], good lubricity [38–40] and outstanding adsorptive desulfurization performance [41]. Besides, BNQDs also exhibit tunable fluorescence emissions, low cytotoxicity, and high environmental friendliness as CQDs [42–45]. Whereas, different from CQDs that undesirable interactions between CQDs and biomolecules exist, which may damage the inherent properties of biomolecules [46], BNQDs show excellent biocompatibility. Therefore, BNQDs has shown great potential in optical imaging, optoelectronics, biomedicine, and other fields in recent years. However, the large-scale fabrication of homogeneous BNQDs in a facile manner is still challenging, which greatly impede their practical applications.

At present, the preparation strategies of BNQDs are mainly divided into two categories: ‘top-down’ and ‘bottom-up’ processes [24]. Based on the latest research progress of BNQDs in current literature, this article summarizes various properties and preparation methods of BNQDs. Subsequently, the corresponding morphologies, yields, and growth mechanisms of these as-obtained BNQDs are discussed in detail. Finally, the potential applications of these as-obtained BNQDs are further discussed. This article is expected to inspire new methods and improvements to achieve large-scale fabrication of homogeneous BNQDs, which will enable their practical applications in future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The properties of boron nitride quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: Biocompatibility and low toxicity are two essential conditions for biological applications of nanomaterials. Compared with conventional heavy metal QDs, such as cadmium sulfide, zinc selenide, and cadmium telluride QDs, BNQDs are environmentally friendly and non-toxic since the main elements of BNQDs are B and N, which will not lead to toxic element leakage in biological applications. This unique characteristic makes the BNQDs be friendly to living organisms and environment. Therefore, they have promising applications in biological analysis, drug delivery, cell imaging, and diagnosis.

Up to date, many results related to the biocompatibility and cytotoxicity of BNQDs have been reported. For example, Allwood group used the MDCKII cells, which are one of the mammalian cells to investigate the cytotoxicity of BNQDs [47]. The results show that a small amount of BNQDs (0–40 μg/mL) exhibit extremely low biotoxicity to MDCKII cells [47]. In addition, Liu et al. further tested the cytotoxicity of BNQDs using the 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay [42]. The survival rates of the untreated He–La cells and those treated with different concentrations (0–350 μg mL−1) of BNQDs were compared [42]. The He–La cell survival rate is 93% when the concentration of BNQDs is 350 μg mL−1, which once again proved that the BNQDs have good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity [42]. Besides, Liu et al. also cultured He–La cells with different concentrations of BNQDs (12.5, 25, 50, and 100 μg mL−1) for 24, 48, and 72 h, respectively, and determined the cell viability of He–La cells by MTT assay [48]. The results show that with the increment of BNQDs concentrations, the cytotoxicity of BNQDs to He–La cells increases slightly, showing a slight decrease in cell survival rates, and when the concentration reaches 100 μg/mL, the cell activity still remains above 75% after 72 h culture [48]. Therefore, it is further demonstrated that BNQDs has low toxicity and good biocompatibility to cells, making BNQDs very promising to be used in the fields of biological analysis, drug delivery, disease diagnosis, and cell imaging, etc.

The photoluminescence phenomenon is the manifestation of quantum effect [49], and it is also the remarkable property of BNQDs. In present study, the researchers mainly attribute the photoluminescence of BNQDs to the quantum confinement effect, which leads to the increase of the gap between conduction band and valence band. However, the specific photoluminescence mechanism remains to be further explored. Currently, the researchers found that the photoluminescence performance of BNQDs is influenced by two factors, which are the size of the as-obtained BNQDs and the defects inside the as-obtained BNQDs, respectively. For example, Liu et al. prepared BNQDs with average lateral sizes of 4.1 ± 0.2, 2.8 ± 0.3, and 2.0 ± 0.2 nm, respectively, using three organic solvents that have different polarities (ethanol (4.3), N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, 6.4) and N-Methyl pyrrolidone (NMP, 10.2)) [42]. The authors found that the BNQDs prepared with ethanol and DMF as solvents (size: 4.1 ± 0.2 and 2.8 ± 0.3 nm) show strong blue fluorescence under the irradiation of violet light, while the BNQDs synthesized in NMP (size: 2.0 ± 0.2 nm) exhibiting relatively high strong green fluorescence [42] (Fig. 2 A–C). This difference can be attributed to the different sizes of the as-obtained BNQDs caused by the different solvent polarities. Greater polarity will lead to stronger interaction between the h-BNNSs and organic solvents, correspondingly resulting in smaller sizes of the as-obtained BNQDs. The smaller sizes will thus lead to a large red shift in the fluorescence emission spectra of the BNQDs (Fig. 2D–I) [42].

In addition, the authors also found that the shift of the photoluminescence of the as-obtained BNQDs is also related to the oxygen content or structural defects in BNQDs [42]. As reported in the literature, the higher the solvent polarity is, the higher the defects of the as-obtained BNQDs will have, resulting in a larger emission wavelength [42]. Besides, the defects induced by the carbon-replaced N vacancy (3-B and 1-B centers) [50,51], carbene structure at zigzag edges [52], and BOx − (x = 1 and 2) species [53] also show a great influence on the photoluminescence of the as-obtained BNQDs. It is estimated that increasing the pH values of the solvents to strong alkalinity will increase the amount of the zigzag edges and weaken the contribution of 1,3 B centers, while decreasing the pH values of the solvents will have the opposite effect, thus leading to different photoluminescence of the as-obtained BNQDs [47]. Li et al. further explored the emission contribution of the zigzag edges and/or from the 1,3 B centers as the pH values vary from 5 to 11 [54]. When the pH value changes to 11 from 5, the observed photoluminescence peak of the as-obtained BNQDs weakens by 29.0% and shifts from 396.8 to 401.1 nm [54]. Conversely, the photoluminescence intensity decreases (additional peak intensity reduction of 8.4%) and shifts to 395.0 nm when the pH values recover from 11 to 5 [54].

Currently, the investigation on the optical properties of BNQDs is still in the preliminary stage, and the wavelength of the emission fluorescence is mainly in the range of 400～550 nm (mainly blue and green fluorescence). Therefore, the photoluminescence of the as-obtained BNQDs at present is relatively single compared with that of GQDs, which exhibit a wide emission wavelength range from violet to red fluorescence [55]. In future studies, more efforts should be paid to the regulation of the photoluminescence of BNQDs. For example, to modify the BNQDs by organic molecules or dope them with different elements including fluoride, chloride, sulfur, and phosphor, etc. to tune the photoluminescence of BNQDs as GQDs, and then expand the applications of BNQDs in different fields.

Electrochemical luminescence (ECL) is a process of converting electric energy into radiant energy. In the ECL process, the electroactive species can be converted into an emissible fluorescent signal by applying the appropriate voltage to the electrode [56,57]. In 2002, after the ECL properties of Si QDs were first reported [58], researchers began to explore the ECL properties of other QDs [59]. As a new kind of QDs, BNQDs are considered as a promising electroluminescent material due to its excellent chemical inertness, good biocompatibility, and easy labeling. In recent years, more and more researchers have not only explored the ECL performance of BNQDs but also regulated and improved ECL performance of BNQDs through various doping and modification. For example, Liu et al. first discovered the ECL properties of BNQDs [42]. At the same time, they also investigated the effect of the type of co-reactants (such as L-cysteine (RSH), K2 S2O8, H2O2, glutathione, and citric acid), pH values, voltage on the ECL intensity of BNQDs (Fig. 3 A,C,D) [42]. The results show that only RSH is the most effective co-reagent for the electrochemical luminescence system of BNQDs, and when the co-reaction agent was 5 mL of RSH, the buffer pH was 7, and the cyclic voltammetry voltage was in the range of −2.0–0 V, the ECL intensity of BNQDs/RSH system is the strongest and highly repeatable [42] (Fig. 3A,C,D). This result indicates that the stability and strength of the ECL signals of BNQDs can be adjusted, so that they could be used in a wide range of sensing and imaging fields.

In addition, researchers also discussed the ECL mechanism of the BNQDs/L-cysteine (RSH) system (Fig. 3B) [42]. The specific reaction mechanism is shown in the formula below [60]: First, the thiol groups on RSH generate RS• radicals (equation (1)) by electrochemical oxidation, and the generated RS• radicals will be further oxidized to RSO2• radicals (equation (2)) by the dissolved oxygen in the solution. Then the RSO2• radicals generated above react with RSH to form RSO• radicals and RSOH (equation (3)). Subsequently, RSO• radicals and RSOH are finally produced through oxidation of solvent oxygen and reduction of RSH. Therefore, through these steps of redox reaction, the anode can produce RS, RSO, RSO2•, RSO3•, and other radicals (equations (4) and (5)). On the other hand, BNQDs take electrons from a platinum plate and produces BNQDs•- (equation (6)). Finally, these three radicals (RSO•, RSO2•, and RSO3•) react with BNQDs•- in an excited state to generate BNQDs through electron transfer (equation (7)) and then emit measurable fluorescence signals (equation (8)) [60]. In 2019, Liu et al. proposed to use thiourea and RSH as different sulfur precursors to regulate the band gap and nanostructure of BNQDs, thus improving their photoelectric performance [61]. Obviously, the BNQDs regulated by two types of S precursor have significantly strong and stable ECL signals, which will further expand the application scope of BNQDs [61].
(1) RSH-e- →RS•+H+
(2) RS•+O2→RSO2 •
(3) RSO2 •+RSH→RSO•+RSOH
(4) RSO•+O2→RSO3 •
(5) RSO3 •+RSH→RSO2 •+RSOH
(6) BNQDs + e−→BNQDs•-
(7) BNQDs•- + RSO•/RSO2 •/RSO3 •→BNQDs•+RSO−/RSO2 -/RSO3 -
(8) BNQDs•→BNQDs + hv

Obviously, ECL is another excellent property of BNQDs, which will further extend the application of them. However, as the photoluminescence of BNQDs, the study on the ECL is also in its initial stage. Therefore, the precise control and regulation of the ECL of BNQDs is still a challenging task. Compared with other QDs, BNQDs have a larger band gap of 5.0–6.0 eV [61], so to reduce or adjust their band gap via edge configuration, chemical functionalization or heteroatomic doping may effectively improve their ECL performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of boron nitride quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, there are two strategies to fabricate BNQDs: ‘top-down’ and ‘bottom-up’ processes as summarized in Fig. 4 . The ‘top-down’ process is related to cut the large-size bulk h-BN into small-size BNQDs by physical exfoliation or chemical tailoring approaches. These approaches mainly include liquid exfoliation, solvothermal/hydrothermal treatment, and ion intercalation method. The mechanism of this process is that certain defects can be produced in h-BN nanosheets (h-BNNSs) by the oxygen-containing functional groups formed in the reaction process, and these defects can be used as the reactive sites to comminute h-BNNSs into BNQDs. The ‘bottom-up’ process is associated with the synthesis of BNQDs by chemical assembly using some simple organic or inorganic materials as precursors under certain conditions. The typical approaches of ‘bottom-up’ process are hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis and microwave irradiation methods. This process has the advantages of easy modification and doping; however, the purity of the as-obtained BNQDs is not enough due to the introduction of heteroatoms. In addition, researchers have also explored some other methods to create BNQDs, such as supercritical fluid (SCF) method, which involves layered bulk h-BN exfoliation and disintegration into BNQDs in SCF processing.

Liquid exfoliation method is a facile, green, and harmless method to prepare BNQDs. This method is usually carried out in liquid solvents with sufficient surface energy [62,63] (such as ethanol, DMF, and NMP). Through liquid exfoliation and subsequent separation, large-size h-BN flakes will be exfoliated and comminuted to form BNQDs. For example, in 2015, Lei et al. prepared BNQDs by facile liquid exfoliation-solvothermal treatment technique [64]. Firstly, the bulk h-BN powders were, respectively, dispersed in DMF and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) to form h-BN/DMF and h-BN/DMSO suspensions, and then the as-obtained suspensions were subjected to an ultrasonication for at least 8 h to exfoliate bulk h-BN powders into high-quality h-BNNSs [64]. In this process, DMF and DMSO were selected as reaction solvents, because they are strongly polar solvents that have the appropriate surface energy to overcome van der Waals forces, which is conducive to the bulk h-BN to be fully exfoliated into thin h-BNNSs [64]. Finally, the as-obtained high-quality h-BNNS suspension were vigorously stirred for 24 h at 140 °C to make the h-BNNSs be incised into BNQDs [64]. The as-obtained BNQDs have an average size of 3.3 nm and thickness of about 2.5 nm with a lattice fringe of 0.215 nm [64]. In addition, the as-obtained BNQDs by this method have good hydrophilicity and can be easily integrated into the polymer matrix to improve the proton exchange performance of the polymer-based membranes, thus enhancing the water retention capacity of the membranes [64].

In addition, Stengl et al. prepared BNQDs by cutting of h-BNNSs prepared by a high-intensity ultrasonication of h-BN [65]. Firstly, the authors prepared bulk layered h-BN with boric acid and urea as precursors, and then the as-obtained bulk layered h-BN was suspended in water and exposed to high-intensity ultrasonication in a pressurized reactor at a pressure of 6 bar for 5 min to peel the bulk h-BN into h-BNNSs with a thickness of less than 1 nm [65]. Finally, BNQDs were successfully prepared by reflux of these exfoliated h-BNNSs in ethylene glycol at 198 °C and atmospheric pressure for 48 h [65]. In this process, the high-intensity ultrasonication can effectively exfoliate the bulk h-BN into thin and few layer nanosheets, providing a good precursor for the preparation of high-quality BNQDs. Similarly, Kumar et al. also prepared BNQDs by using simple high-intensity ultrasonication technique combined with liquid-phase exfoliation as shown in Fig. 5 a [66]. TEM and HRTEM images show that the prepared BNQDs had uniform particle size of 3–6 nm, and an average thickness of 12 atomic h-BN layers [66]. At the same time, the authors also explored the influence of different ultrasonication time (12, 10, 9, and 6 h) on the morphology, structure, and size of BNQDs [66]. Compared with the ultrasonication time of 10, 9, and 6 h, the size of the as-obtained BNQDs by high-intensity ultrasonication at 12 h is smaller, and the particle size distribution is more uniform (Fig. 5b–e) [66]. This may be because the high intensity and long duration of ultrasonication make h-BNNSs easier to be exfoliated, and eventually transforming into BNQDs [66].

Based on the above results, it can be found that liquid exfoliation method is a simple and effective method to prepare uniform BNQDs. It has the advantages of low cost and facile preparation process. However, the problems of long ultrasonication time and low quantum yield limit its further development and application. In addition, the polarity of the solvent used for the preparation of BNQDs has a great effect on the size of the as-obtained BNQDs. Firstly, the high polarity is favorable for weakening the interaction force between two adjacent h-BN layers, thus promoting the exfoliation of the h-BN bulk to form h-BNNSs. Secondly, the bonds between boron and nitrogen have ionicity, so the organic molecules with high polarity are prone to be absorbed by the h-BNNSs. The interaction between the h-BNNSs and organic molecules can also weaken the B–N bond intensity, leading to the fracture of the B–N bonds to form BNQDs under the ultrasonication. Therefore, to find a good solvent with suitable polarity will be the key factor for the liquid exfoliation method to prepare high-quality BNQDs.

Additionally, Xue et al. also cut the bulk h-BN flakes into water-soluble BNQDs with an average size of 3.3 nm by hydrothermal method in the assistance of KOH and NH3
· H2O [73]. Among them, with the aid of KOH and NH3
· H2O, the h-BNNSs are cut into defective structures by hydrothermal treatment, which is more conducive to the formation of smaller BNQDs. Moreover, The as-obtained BNQDs solution shows a strong blue light under 365 nm UV irradiation [73]. The authors attribute the photoluminescence of the as-obtained BNQDs to the defect centers, such as the introduction of BO2 − and C replaced N of N vacancy points [73]. Meanwhile, the as-obtained BNQDs have good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and stable fluorescence properties, which provide the possibility for subsequent biological applications. However, the quantum yield of the as-obtained BNQDs obtained by this process is only 1.8%, which is significantly lower than that reported in the literature (Table 1 ). Following a similar approach, Liu et al. prepared BNQDs by solvothermal treatment with ethanol, DMF and NMP as solvents, respectively [42]. The average size, quantum yield, and luminescence color under Uv–vis (365 nm) of the as-obtained BNQDs become 4.1 ± 0.2 nm, 12.6%, blue light; 2.8 ± 0.3 nm, 16.4%, blue light; and 2.0 ± 0.2 nm, 21.3%, green light, respectively [42]. The authors attribute these differences to the size synergies, surface chemistry, and edge defects of BNQDs from different solvents [42].

Obviously, solvothermal/hydrothermal treatment can be used to prepare BNQDs efficiently. However, the yield and size of the as-obtained BNQDs are also influenced by the polarity of the solvents. Specifically, the higher the polarity of the solvents is, the smaller the size and the greater the red shift of the emission peak of the as-obtained BNQDs will respectively be. Similar to the liquid exfoliation method, to find a good solvent with suitable polarity will also enhance the efficiency of solvothermal/hydrothermal method to prepare high-quality of BNQDs.

Ion intercalation reaction is a process in which ions are reversibly inserted into the crystal gap in order to change the interval between the layers, thus achieving exfoliation. The as-obtained BNQDs by this method have the advantages of small size and thin thickness. At present, Li+, K+, and Na+ are the main intercalation ions. Due to the large gap and weak van der Waals force in h-BN layers, Li+, K+, and Na+ can be inserted into the interlayers to promote the exfoliation of h-BNNSs and then combined with other methods to prepare BNQDs.

Liu et al. simultaneously prepared single-layer h-BNNSs (yield 18.1%) and BNQDs (yield 2.9%) by the ion intercalation method with commercial h-BN powders and NaOH as precursors and ethanol as solvent [48]. Here the selection of ethanol as the solvent can not only make h-BN evenly dispersed in the solvent but also increase the contact area between h-BN powders and NaOH [48]. NaOH is used as the intercalation reagent, because NaOH will corrode the edge of h-BN, making Na+ and OH− more easily intercalated between h-BN layers, and further realizing the exfoliation of h-BN layer by layer. In addition, in the solvothermal process, high temperature and high pressure will weaken the van der Waals force of h-BN powders and further enhance the interaction between NaOH and h-BN powders, which is more conducive to the exfoliation of h-BNNSs and formation of BNQDs [48]. The morphology characterization shows that the as-obtained h-BNNSs by this process was about 1 nm in thickness with a lateral size of about 1.2 μm [48]. Moreover, the BNQDs prepared by this process was uniform in size, with an average particle size of about 5.1 nm and good crystallinity [48].

In addition, Lin et al. prepared single-layer BNQDs by potassium (K) intercalated h-BN layers in 2014 [47]. The specific preparation process is shown in Fig. 8 a: (I) the formation of K-h-BN by K intercalating into h-BN flakes, (II) K-h-BN de-intercalation reaction, and (III) disintegration of h-BN edges under ultrasonication [52,76]. And, as shown in Fig. 8b–g, the monolayer BNQDs with an average size of 10 nm and an average thickness of 0.9 nm were prepared through these steps [47]. In this process, the introduction of the three types of effective luminescent centers (such as, BO2−, carbene zigzag edges, C replaced N vacancy point defects) causes the BNQDs to emerge blue-green luminescence, and the strongest luminescence occurred at 425 nm with nanosecond luminescence attenuation [47]. These as-obtained BNQDs exhibit great application potential in the field of photoelectronics.

Generally, the ion intercalation method provides an effective approach to prepare BNQDs with monolayer structure. However, compared with other preparation methods, the as-obtained BNQDs by this method also have the problems of low quantum yield and large average particle size (Table 1). Here, the efficiency of the ion intercalation method is greatly influenced by the size and properties of the used intercalation ions. Currently, Li+, K+, and Na+ are the main intercalation ions used for inserting into the h-BN interlayers to weaken the interaction force between the adjacent layers. The size and properties of these used intercalation ions will have no change in the following exfoliation and fracture process once they are inserted into the h-BN interlayers. If the ions, such as NH4 +, CO3 2−, and SO4 2−, which can decompose to generate more gases during the following exfoliation and fracture process, it will further weaken the interaction force between the adjacent h-BN layers and promote the exfoliation and fraction of h-BNNSs, thus improving the yield of the as-obtained BNQDs of ion intercalation method. Therefore, to find suitable intercalation ions which can effectively weaken the interaction force between the adjacent h-BN layers will be the key factor for the ion intercalation method to massively fabricate high-quality BNQDs.

The hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis is one of the common methods to prepare various QDs [77,78]. In this method, QDs are prepared under high temperature and pressure with small molecule as precursor and water or organic solvent as reaction medium. For example, as shown in Fig. 9 a, Liu et al. first synthesized hydroxyl and amino functionalized BNQDs by the hydrothermal treatment by using boric acid and ammonia solution as precursors under a nitrogen atmosphere [79]. In the process，the effects of hydrothermal treatment time, the composition of precursors, and the reaction temperatures on the size of BNQDs were also investigated [79]. The results show that the ratio of precursor had little influence on the particle size of BNQDs when the reaction temperature was 180 °C and reaction time was 12 h [79]. In addition, HRTEM and AFM images (Fig. 9b–e) of BNQDs show that the as-obtained BNQDs have a good size distribution with an average size of 2.38 nm, an average thickness of 0.91 nm and a high quantum yield of 18.3% [79]. To further characterize the properties of the as-obtained BNQDs, density functional theory was used to simulate the hydrothermal reaction process and fluorescence quenching mechanism, which further confirmed the method is green and efficient [79]. However, in the process of material selection, ammonia solution as a nitrogen source is volatile and pollutional, which will hinder its further biological application. To avoid this problem, in 2017, Huo et al. selected boric acid and melamine as precursors to synthesize monolayer BNQDs by hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 15 h, and the as-obtained BNQDs show an average size of 3 nm [46]. In this process, green and environmentally friendly melamine was selected as nitrogen source, providing a new material for the preparation of high-quality BNQDs.

Generally, the hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis offers an effective approach to prepare BNQDs with monolayer structure and high yield. In the whole process, nitrogen source selection plays a key role in the synthesis of BNQDs. Therefore, the exploration of low-cost and environmentally friendly precursors will be the main research direction for the preparation of high-quality BNQDs.

Microwave irradiation method is one of the most efficient methods for synthesizing nanomaterials [80]. Compared with other preparation methods, microwave irradiation method has the advantages of simple process, high reaction rate, short synthesis time, and high fluorescence quantum yield. For example, in 2017, Fan et al. prepared high-quality BNQDs by microwave radiation technique under the microwave radiation power of 500 W at 150 °C for 10 min [81]. The subsequent characterization images show that the monolayer h-BNNSs with a lateral size of 300 nm and a uniform BNQDs with an average lateral size of 3.21 nm are successfully prepared [81]. These products show better crystallinity and homogeneity than other reported metal-free QDs [47,82]. Meanwhile, the quantum yield of BNQDs is up to 23.44%, much higher than that reported in the literature in recent years (Table 1). The technique provides an efficient and time-saving method for the preparation of BNQDs with more uniformity and higher quantum yield. In addition, the biggest characteristic of this work is BNQDs with different particle size and dispersion degree can be obtained by controlling the reaction temperature and duration time in the microwave irradiation process. When the microwave radiation temperature is 140, 150, and 160 °C, the corresponding sizes are 4.03, 3.21, and 3.15 nm, respectively, which reveals that the average particle size of BNQDs decreases gradually with the increase of reaction temperature [81]. Similarly, the effect of different radiation time on the size was also investigated. With the increase of reaction time (5, 10, and 25 min), the size was 7.05, 3.21, and 1.98 nm, respectively [81]. Above research results clearly show that the reaction time has a greater effect on the lateral size and distribution than the reaction temperature, which may be due to the increasing microwave irradiation causes the BNQDs molecules to be arranged in an orderly manner, which in turn leads to a decrease in the lateral size of the BNQDs molecules and a more orderly dispersion of the BNQD molecules [81]. In addition, Liu et al. also successfully synthesized sulfur-regulated BNQDs (S-BNQDsT and S-BNQDsL) by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method using boric acid and melamine as precursors and thiourea and L-cysteine as dopants [61].

Based on the above discussion, it can be concluded that the microwave irradiation technique is a simple and efficient method to prepare BNQDs. More importantly, the prepared BNQDs have good quality and uniform distribution, indicating that this method is ideal to prepare BNQDs currently.

SCF is a unique and homogeneous reaction medium. The supercritical state mainly refers to the thermodynamic state that exceeds the critical level by increasing the temperature and pressure of the solvent. In addition, SCFs have been used for the synthesis of various metal nanoparticles and exfoliation and functionalization of graphene nanosheets due to their many unique properties [83–87]. For instance, in 2016, Sathish et al. prepared the average size of 5 nm, and blue-luminescence BNQDs with a quantum yield of 2.8% by SCF method for the first time [88]. Specific steps as shown in Fig. 10 A：The h-BN powders were firstly dispersed in DMF after ultrasonication treatment, following by being sealed in a stainless steel reactor and reacted at 450 °C for 40 min [88]. After the reaction, the suspension was quenched quickly in ice cold water, and finally, the BNQDs can be successfully obtained by filtration with a microporous membrane [88]. The HRTEM and AFM image (Fig. 10B-C) show that the SCF process could effectively exfoliate and incise the layered h-BN powders into BNQDs with uniform size [88]. From the above discussion, it can be found that the SCF process is novel, simple, and fast to prepare BNQDs; however, it still has the problem of low quantum yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of boron nitride quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: BNQDs not only have similar physical and chemical properties to traditional QDs but also exhibit the unique advantages of low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and stable fluorescence properties. Therefore, it could replace the traditional QDs and become a powerful material for biological research. In 2015, Lei et al. successfully applied the as-obtained BNQDs to mark He–La cells [64]. He–La cells were firstly incubated in medium (DMEM, high glucose) with BNQDs for 7 h at 37 °C, and bright fluorescence of He–La cells was observed by laser confocal fluorescence microscopy [64]. Moreover, through experimental observation, there was a good overlap between the fluorescence of Lyso Tracker (Lysosome red fluorescent probe) and BNQDs probe in cells, which implies that BNQDs may enter the cell body due to the endocytosis, and the QDs entered the cytoplasm through the cell membrane rather than the nuclei [64]. Therefore, when BNQDs are used for cell labels, they do not disrupt the destruction of gene sequences, which further proved that the as-obtained BNQDs have low cytotoxicity [64]. Similarly, Allwood group stained the MDCKII cells using BNQDs and DAPI (4,6-diamidino-2-phenylic-dole) to illustrate the potential application of BNQDs in bio-imaging [47]. Fig. 11 shows the confocal microscopy images of MDCKII cells staining with BNQDs and DAPI, respectively. Comparing the difference in the color of cells, it can be found that BNQDs have good luminescence performance, and can be used in high-contrast biological imaging [47].

The excellent performance of BNQDs makes it a promising fluorescent staining. For example, Liu et al. used the BNQDs solution for cotton and silk fibers staining [42]. The fluorescence microscope demonstrates that the cotton after staining are green and red under the excitation of blue (450–480 nm) and green (510–550 nm) light, respectively (Fig. 12 A) [42]. Meanwhile, Huo et al. measured the change of fluorescence intensity of cotton fibers stained by BNQDs with time. Fig. 12B (a-c) and (d-f) show the fluorescence images of cotton fibers stained by BNQDs in 0 and 14 days, respectively [46]. Obviously, the results show that the fluorescence intensity of the stained cotton fiber remains basically unchanged after 14 days, which proves the good stability of BNQDs [46]. Therefore, BNQDs are promising in the applications of biological and chemical staining.

Metal ions, such as Ni2+, Fe2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+, have unimaginable harm to water environment and human health. Therefore, the development of a high selectivity, good sensitivity, and low-cost detection method to achieve quantitative detection of metal ions, has been the focus of chemical research. As a kind of QDs with excellent optical performance, BNQDs exhibit great potential in ion detection. In 2017, Huo et al. developed a BNQD fluorescence sensing system for Fe3+ detection (Fig. 13 ) [46]. As shown in Fig. 13a–b, the changes of the photoluminescence in the presence of different metal ions such as Al3+,Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, and Ag+ were investigated [46]. The results show that when the excitation wavelengths are 230 and 300 nm, BNQDs show obvious fluorescence quenching phenomenon after adding Fe3+ [46]. Whereas, no obvious fluorescence quenching phenomenon can be observed when other metal ions are added [46]. Fig. 13c and e further shows the high sensitivity of the as-obtained BNQDs to the detection of Fe3+ by detecting the fluorescence intensity at the excitation wavelength of 230 and 300 nm [46]. Fig. 13d and f shows a good linear relationship between the photoluminescence quenching efficiency (I/I0) and Fe3+ concentration of BNQDs at wavelengths of 230 and 300 nm [46]. In addition, based on previous studies, the mechanism of photoluminescence quenching is also introduced in detail, as follows: the fluorescence quenching of BNQDs may be attributed to the special binding force between Fe3+ and the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of BNQDs, which facilitates the transfer of the excited electrons on BNQDs. The resulting non-radiative electron/hole recombination directly leads to an obvious fluorescence quenching of BNQDs [89,90]. Therefore, BNQDs can be used as fluorescence sensing agent and is expected to be used to detect Fe3+ in aquatic environment and biomedical fields.

In addition, Liu et al. discussed the impact of Ni2+, Fe2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+ and other metal ions on the change of fluorescence intensity of BNQDs [42]. By comparison, it is confirmed that BNQDs exhibited a high selectivity, a wide linear range, and a low detection limit for the detection of Fe3+ [91]. Furthermore, Yao et al. also developed a rapid fluorescence quenching method based on the BNQDs for the determination of Ni2+by photo-induced electron transfer [92]. Under the optimal conditions, the determination of Ni2+ in natural water samples could be completed by using the light-induced translocation of BNQDs for 5 min, and the detection limit was 0.1 mol L−1 [92]. This method is simpler and faster than the traditional method of detecting Ni2+ (such as organic molecular colorimetry and high-performance liquid chromatograph) [93–95].

Based on the strong and stable optical properties of BNQDs, they can also be used as a fluorescence sensor to detect some highly toxic compounds scattered in water and land that do great harm to human body. For example, 2,4, 6-trinitrophenol (TNP) is a highly toxic and explosive compound that causes unimaginable harm to humans and the environment [96]. Therefore, sensitive and rapid detection of TNP and its analogues is of great importance. In 2018, Peng et al. first prepared BNQDs with stable fluorescence, good salt tolerance, and excellent antiphotobleaching capability by solvothermal treatment method [96]. Then, based on strong inner filter effect(IFE) between TNP and the BNQDs, BNQD based turn-off sensor is constructed, which can be used to detect TNP rapidly and selectively [96]. To investigate the mechanism of fluorescent quenching of BNQDS by TPN, the time-resolved photoluminescence spectra and absorption spectra of BNQD and BNQDs/TNP systems were investigated as shown in Fig. 14 b and c, respectively [96]. The results show that the fluorescence lifetimes and absorption spectra of the two systems are almost greatly changed, indicating that there is no obvious charge transfer between BNQDs and TNP [96]. Therefore, the author thinks that the main reason of TNP quenching BNQDs fluorescence may be the strong IFE between TNP and BNQDs [96]. As shown in Fig. 14d, under the excitation of 300 nm, BNQDs exhibit strong fluorescence emission at 320–550 nm, and most of them overlapped with the absorption spectrum of TNP in a wide range, which further verifies its quenching mechanism [96]. Moreover, the sensor has high sensitivity as shown in Fig. 14e–f that the fluorescence intensity of BNQDs decreases gradually as TNP is gradually added into the reaction system, and the F0/F value is in a linear relationship with TNP in the range of 0–50 μM [96]. Besides, the sensor can also be used to detect some TPN analogues (PHE, DNP, TNT, and DNT) and some common metal ions (Ag+, Cu2+, Fe 2+, Fe3+, and NO3 -) as shown in Fig. 14g [96]. Based on the above discussion, the fluorescence sensor based on BNQDs exhibits great potential for sensitive and selective detection of TNP, TPN analogues, and some common metal ions in water.

In recent years, genetic detection has been playing an increasingly important role in the early diagnosis, efficacy evaluation, and postoperative monitoring of some diseases and has become one of the hot spots in the contemporary medical field. For example, Liu et al. constructed an ECL sensing system based on surface plasmon coupled-ECL (SPC-ECL) mechanism for detecting BRAF genes using S-BNQDs [61]. The sensing system is mainly composed of a target DNA and hairpin DNAs (H1 and H2). The capture of two hairpin DNAs (H1 and H2) and BRAF will eventually lead to multiple H1–H2 duplex being immobilized on the electrode after multiple cycles [61]. When gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) were used to label with H2 to form a signal probe, the signal probe would be adsorbed on the S-BNQDs, and the resonance between the two causes the SPC-ECL to achieve continuous amplification of QD-ECL signal [61]. As shown in Fig. 15 A-H, ECL intensity enhancement is also monitored by using a smartphone. Based on SPC-ECL effect, the ECL intensity of S-BNQDs significantly increases with the increase of target DNA concentration [61]. Meanwhile, ECL intensity and BRAF gene concentration are also investigated, and it is found that the established DNA sensing system could quantify BRAF gene 1 pmol/L to 1.5 nmol/L, and its detection limit was 0.3 pmol/L (Fig. 15I-J) [61]. Therefore, this kind of ECL sensor based on S-BNQDs has great application potential in medical field in future. Moreover, in 2020, Qin et al. selected BNQDs as the coreactant of rGO@Au@Ru–SiO2 nanocomposite to enhance the ECL signal of its composite material, and constructed an ECL immunosensor for the detection of alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) based on rGO@Au@Ru–SiO2/BNQDs system [97]. The authors used proposed ECL immunosensor to detect AFP and found that the constructed ECL sensor exhibits good stability, high selectivity, good specificity, and excellent reproducibility to AFP antigens [97]. Therefore, the proposed method provides an efficient method for the determination of actual APF antigens.

Following CQDs and GQDs, BNQDs have also been favored by researchers because of their advantages of luminescence performance, good biocompatibility, and low biotoxicity. In this article, we summarize the latest research progress in the field of BNQDs, focusing on their properties, preparation methods, and applications in cell imaging, fluorescence staining, metal ion detection, compound detection, and genetic detection. However, at present, there are still some problems in the properties, preparation, and application of BNQDs:
a)
How to achieve a simple and scale-up method to controllably prepare high-quality BNQDs with uniform size and high quantum yield remains to be further explored. In future research, the improvement should be concentrated at three aspects: 1) The selection of solvents is the key factor for the preparation of high-quality BNQDs. The solvents with high polarity are prone to prepare BNQDs with small size and good dispersion. 2) The ions of NH4 +, CO3 2−, and SO4 2−, etc., which can decompose to generate gases during the following exfoliation process can be selected as intercalation reagents, since these ions can decompose to generate NH3, CO2, and SO2. During the gas releasing process, the van der Waals forces between adjacent h-BN layers will be further weakened, thus promoting the exfoliation and fraction of h-BNNSs. 3) The main tasks of preparing high-quality BNQD by bottom up methods is to continuously explore low-cost, high-efficient, and environmentally friendly precursors.
b)
Currently, the investigations on the optical properties of BNQDs including photoluminescence and ECL are still in the preliminary stage. More work should be done to improve the optical properties of BNQDs in future. The modification of BNQDs by organic molecules or doping the BNQDs with different elements, such as fluoride, chloride, sulfur, and phosphor, may be effective strategies to tune the photoluminescence or ELC properties of BNQDs. In addition, the deep investigation of the luminescence mechanisms of BNQDs should also be performed.
c)
Although BNQDs are widely used in fields such as bioimaging and cell labeling, they only remain in the research stage of mice or in vitro cells. Therefore, how to realize in vivo labeling and practical application in biomedical field will be the main research object.

On the whole, compared with some other QDs, the research on BNQDs is still in its initial stage, and its applications in many fields have not yet been covered (such as drug delivery [98,99], photocatalytic [100], adsorption [101,102] etc.). Therefore, more new methods and improvements should be done to achieve large-scale fabrication of homogeneous BNQDs with enhanced photoluminescence, ECL, and biological properties, etc., thus expanding their practical applications in future.
